nassau_bahamas.blurb: Nassau is the Caribbean's busiest cruise port, and the rebuilt harbourfront makes it an easy self-guided day. Downtown, the Queen's Staircase and Fort Fincastle are a short walk from the ship, with Junkanoo Beach ten minutes the other way.
miss: The Queen's Staircase — 66 steps hand-carved through limestone
Junkanoo Beach, the free walk-to beach west of the port
John Watling's Distillery for a shaded rum stop
nassau_bahamas.tip: Skip the pushy taxi rank quotes — everything listed above is walkable, and Paradise Island water taxis are cheaper than cabs.

freeport_bahamas.blurb: Freeport is an industrial harbour, so the day is about where you go from it rather than the port itself. Most ships run shuttles to Port Lucaya Marketplace; the real prizes are the beaches and Lucayan National Park's caves.
miss: Gold Rock Beach at low tide — huge ribbed sandbars
Lucayan National Park's underwater cave system
Port Lucaya Marketplace for a low-effort browse
freeport_bahamas.tip: There's nothing to see on foot at the pier — decide between beach and park before you disembark and share a taxi to cut the fare.

bimini_bahamas.blurb: Tiny Bimini was Hemingway's fishing hideout and still feels like a sandbar with a marina attached. Ships dock at the north end; free trams shuttle to Radio Beach and the Resorts World complex with its pool and beach clubs.
miss: Radio Beach's shallow, glass-clear swimming
The Dolphin House mosaic museum in Alice Town
A conch salad shack — watch it made to order
bimini_bahamas.tip: The whole island is about seven miles long — a golf-cart rental covers all of it in an afternoon if the beach isn't enough.

coco_cay.blurb: Perfect Day at CocoCay is Royal Caribbean's private island, and everything runs on your cruise card. The free side is genuinely good — Chill Island's beaches, the pool, and included lunch — while the waterpark and Coco Beach Club are paid extras.
miss: Oasis Lagoon, the Caribbean's largest freshwater pool
Chill Island's included loungers and calm swimming
The Up, Up and Away balloon view if it's flying
coco_cay.tip: Book waterpark passes before the cruise if you want them — onboard prices climb, and on sold-out days they vanish entirely.

castaway_cay.blurb: Disney's Castaway Cay is the slickest private island operation afloat — the ship docks right at the pier and trams run the length of the beach. Family beaches, the adults-only Serenity Bay, and included barbecue lunch make it a zero-effort day.
miss: Serenity Bay if you're travelling without kids
Snorkelling the lagoon's sunken 'hidden Mickey' props
The 5K fun run before the beach fills up
castaway_cay.tip: Bikes and floats sell out at the rental hut — reserve online pre-cruise, and walk one tram stop further for quieter sand.

half_moon_cay.blurb: Half Moon Cay is Holland America and Carnival's shared private island, and its crescent beach is regularly rated the best in the Bahamas — two miles of powder with no buildings behind it. It's a tender port, so the day rewards an early start.
miss: The first stretch of the crescent for calm, shallow water
Horseback riding that ends with a swim in the sea
The walk to the quiet far end past the cabanas
half_moon_cay.tip: Tender queues stack up after breakfast — be on an early one and you'll have an hour of near-empty beach before the crowd lands.

princess_cays.blurb: Princess Cays sits on the southern tip of Eleuthera, a straightforward beach-and-barbecue day split into two halves — the busier main beach by the tender pier and a quieter stretch beyond the water-sports hut. Everything charges to your cruise card.
miss: The quieter beach south of the pier
Snorkelling the rocky points at either end
The included beach barbecue — go early or late
princess_cays.tip: Shade is the scarce resource here, not sand — grab a spot under the trees or book a clamshell as soon as you land.

great_stirrup_cay.blurb: Norwegian's Great Stirrup Cay finally has a pier, ending decades of tender lotteries. The island is a classic Bahamas beach day — included barbecue, clear snorkelling around the rocks, and paid upgrades from cabanas to the Silver Cove lagoon area.
miss: Snorkelling around Bertha's Beach rocks
The lighthouse-end walk away from the crowds
The underwater sculpture garden just offshore
great_stirrup_cay.tip: With the new pier, port days here run longer and fuller than they used to — claim loungers early, the island fills fast now.

ocean_cay_msc.blurb: Ocean Cay is MSC's converted industrial sand-mining island, now a marine reserve — and it shows in the water clarity. The ship stays docked late into the evening on most calls, with the lighthouse show after dark as the signature moment.
miss: The lighthouse light show after sunset
North Beach for the calmest, clearest swimming
The evening on-island time most other private islands don't offer
ocean_cay_msc.tip: This is the rare private island where ships stay until late — plan a shore dinner-and-lightshow evening rather than cramming it all in by 4pm.

celebration_key.blurb: Celebration Key is Carnival's purpose-built Grand Bahama port, opened in 2025 and designed around five 'portals' — from the free Paradise Plaza lagoon to adults-only and family zones. It's less castaway island, more polished beach resort at scale.
miss: The huge freshwater lagoons — included, unlike much here
Starfish Lagoon's calmer family end
The suspended bridge view over Paradise Plaza
celebration_key.tip: It's big — pick your portal before you walk off and use the trams, because trudging the full length in midday heat gets old fast.

grand_cayman.blurb: George Town is one of the Caribbean's last big tender ports, but the tenders land you straight in the walkable duty-free centre. The island's headline acts — Seven Mile Beach and the Stingray City sandbar — are both short, cheap rides away.
miss: Stingray City — waist-deep water, wild southern stingrays
Seven Mile Beach's public access at Governor's Beach
Cayman Spirits distillery tour near the pier
grand_cayman.tip: Public buses to Seven Mile run constantly from downtown for a couple of dollars — no need for the taxi rank or a ship excursion just to reach the sand.

ocho_rios_jamaica.blurb: Ocho Rios is Jamaica's original cruise town, with Dunn's River Falls — the island's most famous excursion — just five minutes from the pier. The town itself is walkable, but the day really belongs to the falls, gardens and river adventures around it.
miss: Climbing Dunn's River Falls (water shoes essential)
Mystic Mountain's rainforest bobsled ride
Konoko Falls for a quieter falls alternative
ocho_rios_jamaica.tip: Dunn's River is heaving when the ships are in — book the earliest slot you can or flip the day and go after 1pm when the tour groups thin out.

falmouth_jamaica.blurb: Falmouth's purpose-built port opens straight into one of the Caribbean's best-preserved Georgian towns. It's a base for the same excursions as Ocho Rios and Montego Bay, but the town's history walk is a genuinely good free hour.
miss: The Falmouth Heritage Walk's Georgian architecture
Glistening Waters' bioluminescent lagoon (evening calls)
Martha Brae river rafting, thirty minutes away
falmouth_jamaica.tip: The craft market inside the port gates charges port prices — walk two streets into the real town for the same items and friendlier haggling.

montego_bay_jamaica.blurb: Montego Bay is Jamaica's second city, and the cruise terminal sits in the industrial freeport — so plan on transport. Doctor's Cave Beach and the Hip Strip are ten minutes away, with rafting, great houses and luminous lagoon trips further out.
miss: Doctor's Cave Beach's famously clear water
Rose Hall Great House and its ghost story
Rafting the Martha Brae on a bamboo raft
montego_bay_jamaica.tip: Agree taxi fares before getting in — rates to Doctor's Cave are posted at the terminal, and returning drivers will find you at the beach gate at pickup time.

grand_turk.blurb: Grand Turk is the rare port where the beach starts literally at the end of the pier — white sand, calm water and the cruise centre's pool all within barefoot distance. Beyond it lies a sleepy island of wild donkeys and one of the world's best walls for diving.
miss: The pier-side beach — no transport needed at all
Gibbs Cay stingray sandbar trip
Cockburn Town's weathered colonial waterfront
grand_turk.tip: You genuinely don't need an excursion here for a great day — the free beach by the ship rivals anything you'd pay to reach.

amber_cove.blurb: Amber Cove is Carnival's purpose-built gateway to the Dominican Republic's north coast, with a big pool complex and zip line inside the port itself. Outside the gates, Puerto Plata's cable car, waterfalls and rum heritage make the case for venturing out.
miss: The Teleférico cable car up Mount Isabel de Torres
The 27 Waterfalls of Damajagua canyoning trip
Puerto Plata's Victorian old town and amber museum
amber_cove.tip: The port pool is free and genuinely good — if you've done the DR before, a zero-spend pool day here is a legitimate choice.

labadee_haiti.blurb: Labadee is Royal Caribbean's fenced private peninsula on Haiti's north coast — a mountainous, dramatic setting unlike the flat Bahamas islands. Beaches, the huge Dragon's Breath zip line and an included barbecue fill the day.
miss: Dragon's Breath — 2,600ft of zip line over the sea
Columbus Cove's calm, shaded swimming
The artisan market for Haitian metalwork (haggle kindly)
labadee_haiti.tip: Nellie's Beach and Columbus Cove are calmer and prettier than the main Adrenaline Beach — turn right off the pier, not left.

st_thomas_usvi.blurb: Charlotte Amalie combines duty-free shopping (US visitors get a raised allowance here) with one of the Caribbean's prettiest harbours. Magens Bay's postcard beach and the Skyride viewpoint are the classic half-days; St John's national park beaches are the upgrade.
miss: Magens Bay — the beach on all the posters
The Skyride to Paradise Point above the harbour
A ferry side-trip to Trunk Bay on St John
st_thomas_usvi.tip: Open-air safari taxis run fixed per-person rates to Magens Bay — cheaper than tours, and the 99 Steps in town fill the gap if you split the day.

st_maarten.blurb: Philipsburg gives you two countries in one port day — the Dutch side's beaches and shopping, and the French side's food and style over the open border. The famous jet-blast beach at Maho sits under the airport approach twenty minutes away.
miss: Maho Beach's landing jets, close enough to touch
The water taxi across Great Bay to Front Street
Lunch in Grand Case on the French side
st_maarten.tip: Check the arrivals board before committing to Maho — the show is only as good as the flight schedule, and the big jets cluster around midday.

san-juan.blurb: Old San Juan is one of cruising's great walk-off ports — five hundred years of blue cobblestones, pastel facades and two vast Spanish fortresses, all starting a few steps from the gangway. No transport, no plan needed; just walk uphill and get lost.
miss: El Morro fortress and its kite-flying lawn
Calle Fortaleza's colour and the old city gates
A barrachina piña colada at its claimed birthplace
san-juan.tip: Do the forts early — the walls give no shade and the trade wind on El Morro's lawn is the town's best free air conditioning.

tortola_bvi.blurb: Road Town is the low-key capital of the British Virgin Islands, and the port is really a springboard — to Cane Garden Bay over the mountain, to the Baths' giant boulders on Virgin Gorda, or just to a beach bar with a painkiller cocktail.
miss: The Baths on Virgin Gorda — worth the ferry
Cane Garden Bay's beach-bar crescent
The mountain-road viewpoints en route to either
tortola_bvi.tip: If the Baths are the goal, book a tour or early ferry immediately on landing — independent ferry slots on ship days are the island's scarcest commodity.

antigua.blurb: Antigua claims a beach for every day of the year, and the ships dock right in St John's among the shops and rum bars. Nelson's Dockyard — the world's only continuously working Georgian dockyard — anchors the far side of the island.
miss: Nelson's Dockyard and Shirley Heights' harbour view
Valley Church or Darkwood Beach on the calm west coast
Fig Tree Drive through the rainforest interior
antigua.tip: Shirley Heights is famous for Sunday sunset parties, but the view is free and empty at midday — pair it with the Dockyard in one taxi loop.

st_kitts.blurb: St Kitts packs a lot into a small island — a UNESCO fortress on Brimstone Hill, the scenic railway built for the sugar crop, and green vervet monkeys that outnumber people. Port Zante lands you straight into compact, walkable Basseterre.
miss: Brimstone Hill Fortress, the 'Gibraltar of the West Indies'
The St Kitts Scenic Railway's coastal loop
Timothy Hill's two-ocean viewpoint
st_kitts.tip: Timothy Hill's photo stop looks over Atlantic and Caribbean at once — every island taxi tour includes it, so a shared cab beats a formal excursion on price.

st_lucia.blurb: St Lucia is the drama queen of the eastern Caribbean — the twin Piton peaks, a drive-in volcano and rainforest all crowd the south while ships dock in workaday Castries. It's a big island; the best of it needs a committed excursion day.
miss: The Pitons from Soufrière — by boat beats by road
Sulphur Springs' drive-in volcano and mud baths
Marigot Bay for a shorter, prettier half-day
st_lucia.tip: Soufrière is 90 winding minutes each way by road — take a catamaran down the coast instead and you get the Pitons from the water plus the drive back skipped.

dominica.blurb: Dominica calls itself the Nature Island and means it — no big resorts, just rainforest, 365 rivers, boiling volcanic springs and the Caribbean's best whale watching. Ships dock in the middle of Roseau's charmingly weathered waterfront.
miss: Trafalgar Falls' twin cascades and hot pools
Titou Gorge's swim-through canyon
Champagne Reef, snorkelling over volcanic bubbles
dominica.tip: This is the port to spend your excursion budget on — the interior is the entire point of Dominica and it doesn't reveal itself from the pier.

grenada.blurb: St George's horseshoe harbour is arguably the prettiest port town in the Caribbean, and the whole island smells faintly of its nutmeg crop. Grand Anse beach is ten minutes away; waterfalls and spice estates fill the interior.
miss: Grand Anse's two miles of calm, resort-free beach
The Underwater Sculpture Park by snorkel or boat
Fort George's harbour panorama at the top of town
grenada.tip: Water taxis shuttle from the port straight across the bay to Grand Anse for a few dollars — quicker and far more scenic than the road.

barbados.blurb: Bridgetown is the polished pro of southern Caribbean cruising — a UNESCO-listed capital, swimmable west-coast water, and the birthplace of rum with the distillery tours to prove it. The port is a working harbour; a shuttle covers the gate run.
miss: Swimming with turtles in Carlisle Bay
Mount Gay's rum distillery tour
Harrison's Cave in the island's limestone core
barbados.tip: Carlisle Bay's beach and turtle-swim boats are barely ten minutes from the ship — an independent taxi-and-beach day here is as easy as the Caribbean gets.

aruba.blurb: Aruba sits outside the hurricane belt with a near-guaranteed forecast: sunny, 28 degrees, windy. Oranjestad's Dutch-colonial pastels start at the pier, Eagle and Palm beaches run up the west coast, and the cactus-strewn national park fills the wild east.
miss: Eagle Beach and its famous fofoti trees
The free tram loop through Oranjestad
Arikok National Park's natural pool by jeep
aruba.tip: The constant trade wind makes the heat deceptive — you'll burn faster than you feel. The bus to Eagle Beach costs a fraction of the taxi rank.

curacao.blurb: Willemstad's rainbow-coloured Handelskade waterfront is the Caribbean's most photographed street, and the floating Queen Emma pontoon bridge swings open for ships right beside it. Beyond town lie thirty-plus cove beaches tucked into the coast.
miss: The Queen Emma bridge swinging open
Punda and Otrobanda's UNESCO streets either side of it
Playa Kenepa's cliff-backed double cove (worth the drive)
curacao.tip: When the pontoon bridge opens for traffic, free ferries shuttle across instead — riding one is the best two-minute harbour tour in the Caribbean.

bonaire.blurb: Bonaire is a marine park with an island attached — the entire coast is protected, and the shore diving and snorkelling are the best you can reach on foot in the Caribbean. Kralendijk is tiny, tidy and starts at the gangway.
miss: Snorkelling straight off Te Amo or Bachelor's Beach
Klein Bonaire's reef by water taxi
The pink salt flats and flamingos of the south loop
bonaire.tip: Rent snorkel gear in town and the shore entry points do the rest — this is the one port where the DIY option genuinely beats the boat tours.

kings_wharf_bermuda.blurb: Bermuda's Royal Naval Dockyard is both the cruise terminal and an attraction in its own right — a Victorian fortress complex of museums, craft markets and the National Museum. Pink-sand Horseshoe Bay and the ferry to Hamilton complete the trio.
miss: Horseshoe Bay's pink sand (bus or minibus shuttle)
The National Museum inside the Keep's ramparts
The fast ferry across the Great Sound to Hamilton
kings_wharf_bermuda.tip: Buy a transport pass at the Dockyard ferry terminal — it covers buses and ferries island-wide and pays for itself in one Hamilton round trip.

cozumel_mexico.blurb: Cozumel is the western Caribbean's heavyweight — often three or four ships at once — thanks to the reef diving that made Jacques Cousteau famous here and its ferry link to the Mayan mainland. San Miguel town is walkable from the downtown pier.
miss: Snorkelling or diving Palancar and Columbia reefs
Punta Sur eco park at the island's wild tip
El Cielo's starfish sandbar by boat
cozumel_mexico.tip: Check which pier you're on — Punta Langosta walks straight into town, while Puerta Maya and the International pier need a short fixed-rate taxi.

costa_maya_mahahual_mexico.blurb: Costa Maya is a purpose-built port on a once-remote stretch of coast, with a pool-and-shops village at the pier and the fishing town of Mahahual a short ride south. Its trump card is proximity to the seldom-crowded Chacchoben Mayan ruins.
miss: Chacchoben's jungle-wrapped pyramids
Mahahual's malecón beach clubs
The Bacalar seven-colour lagoon if you have a long day
costa_maya_mahahual_mexico.tip: Mahahual's beach clubs will collect you free from the port if you commit to a lunch minimum — a cheaper, calmer day than the pier-side pool crush.

belize_city_belize.blurb: Belize City is a long tender ride and not the destination itself — the country behind it is. This is the jumping-off point for cave tubing through jungle river caves, Mayan ruins at Altun Ha and Lamanai, and the world's second-largest barrier reef.
miss: Cave tubing the Nohoch Che'en caves
Lamanai's ruins reached by river safari
Snorkelling the barrier reef off Caye Caulker
belize_city_belize.tip: Tenders here eat 30–45 minutes each way — book something substantial and organised rather than planning to wander, because there's little to wander to.

roatan_honduras.blurb: Roatán sits on the Mesoamerican reef and delivers the best-value snorkelling and diving in the western Caribbean. Ships dock at Mahogany Bay or Coxen Hole; West Bay's white-sand beach with reef swimming straight off it is the classic day.
miss: West Bay Beach — reef snorkelling from the sand
Little French Key's island day resort
The sloth and monkey sanctuaries (with expectations set)
roatan_honduras.tip: Mahogany Bay's chairlift to its private beach is fine, but West Bay is the beach the island is famous for — share a taxi and agree the return time.

cartagena_colombia.blurb: Cartagena's walled old town is one of the great colonial cities of the Americas — flower-draped balconies, plazas and 400-year-old ramparts, all UNESCO-listed. The port itself hides a surprise: a free aviary-and-monkey garden inside the terminal.
miss: The walled city's streets around Plaza Santo Domingo
San Felipe fortress, the largest Spanish fort in the Americas
The port terminal's own flamingos and toucans
cartagena_colombia.tip: It's seriously hot and humid by mid-morning — do the walls first, retreat to a shaded café by noon, and keep small peso notes for street vendors.

colon_panama.blurb: Colón exists on cruise itineraries for one reason: the Panama Canal is up the road. Partial transits, the Agua Clara visitor centre overlooking the new locks, and rainforest boat safaris on Gatún Lake are the day's currency — the city itself isn't for wandering.
miss: Agua Clara locks viewing platform
Gatún Lake boat safari among monkey islands
The Panama Canal Railway's ocean-to-ocean run
colon_panama.tip: This is a book-something port — independent wandering isn't rewarding or advisable here, but the canal excursions genuinely live up to the billing.

puntarenas_costa_rica.blurb: Puntarenas is Costa Rica's Pacific cruise door, a long sandspit town that serves as launchpad to the country's real riches — cloud forests, zip lines, volcanoes and wildlife-thick rivers. Pura vida starts about thirty minutes inland.
miss: Ziplining or hanging bridges in the rainforest canopy
A Tárcoles River crocodile safari
Sarchí's painted oxcarts and coffee country
puntarenas_costa_rica.tip: Distances deceive here — Monteverde and Arenal are too far for a port day, so aim for Tárcoles, Carara National Park or canopy tours within an hour.

panama-city.blurb: Panama City calls (usually at Fuerte Amador) pair the canal's Miraflores locks with a skyline the rest of Central America can't match, plus the colonial Casco Viejo quarter. It's often a partial-transit bookend, so check your ship's timings.
miss: Miraflores locks visitor centre
Casco Viejo's plazas and rooftop bars
The Amador Causeway's skyline view
panama-city.tip: Traffic in Panama City is genuinely bad — build slack into any independent plan and treat all-aboard time with more respect than usual.

key_west_florida_usa.blurb: Key West is a perfect walking port — Duval Street, Hemingway's six-toed cats, the Southernmost Point buoy and Mallory Square are all within a flat mile of the pier. It's the Caribbean's atmosphere on American convenience.
miss: The Hemingway Home and its 60-odd cats
Duval Street end to end, bar by bar
Key lime pie — the frozen chocolate-dipped kind on a stick
key_west_florida_usa.tip: The Southernmost Point buoy queue is pure photo theatre — go before 10am or accept the wait. The Conch Train covers the same ground your feet can.

miami_florida_usa.blurb: PortMiami is the world's busiest cruise port, and if you're staying pre- or post-cruise the city rewards it — South Beach's Art Deco district, Little Havana's cafecito windows, and Wynwood's street-art blocks are each a short ride apart.
miss: The Art Deco district along Ocean Drive
Little Havana's Calle Ocho and a proper Cuban sandwich
Wynwood Walls' warehouse-district murals
miami_florida_usa.tip: If you're flying in for a cruise, come a day early — Miami traffic and flight delays are the classic embarkation-day heartbreakers.

port_everglades_fort_lauderdale_florida_.blurb: Port Everglades in Fort Lauderdale is one of cruising's big three Florida homeports, fifteen minutes from its own airport. The city's canals earn the 'Venice of America' tag, and the beach promenade is a fine pre-cruise wind-down.
miss: A water taxi loop through the canal mansions
Las Olas Boulevard's restaurant row
Fort Lauderdale Beach's wave-wall promenade
port_everglades_fort_lauderdale_florida_.tip: FLL airport is so close you can be ship-to-terminal in 15 minutes — but morning-of flights before 11am are still gambling with disembarkation queues.

port_canaveral_florida_usa.blurb: Port Canaveral is the cruise gateway to Orlando's parks — but its own neighbour is the better story: Kennedy Space Center is twenty minutes away, and rocket launches are now frequent enough that one might share your sail-away.
miss: Kennedy Space Center and the Atlantis shuttle
Cocoa Beach and its famous pier
A launch, if the schedule gods smile
port_canaveral_florida_usa.tip: Check the launch schedule for your embarkation or return date — watching a rocket climb out over the Atlantic from a ship's deck is a genuine bucket-list freebie.

tampa_florida_usa.blurb: Tampa's cruise terminals sit right in the downtown Channel District, an easy walk or streetcar ride from the aquarium, Sparkman Wharf and the Riverwalk. Historic Ybor City — the cigar quarter with free-roaming chickens — is a streetcar away.
miss: Ybor City's cigar-era streets by free streetcar
The Florida Aquarium beside the terminals
The Riverwalk to Armature Works food hall
tampa_florida_usa.tip: The TECO streetcar from the port to Ybor City is free — Tampa is the rare homeport where a car-free pre-cruise day works perfectly.

galveston_texas_usa.blurb: Galveston is the Gulf's boom cruise port, and its Victorian Strand district — a legacy of the days it rivalled New Orleans — starts blocks from the terminals. The seawall beaches and Moody Gardens' pyramids fill out a pre-cruise day.
miss: The Strand's iron-front Victorian blocks
The 1877 tall ship Elissa at the seaport museum
Moody Gardens' rainforest pyramid
galveston_texas_usa.tip: Houston's airports are 45–90 minutes away depending on traffic — book transfers with margin, because the causeway is the only road onto the island.

new-orleans.blurb: Cruising from New Orleans means the French Quarter is essentially part of the port — the terminal sits at the foot of the CBD, a streetcar or short walk from beignets, brass bands and the Mississippi's crescent. Come early, stay late.
miss: Café du Monde beignets in Jackson Square's shadow
Frenchmen Street for the locals' live music
A St Charles streetcar ride under the oaks
new-orleans.tip: The ship sails down 100 miles of Mississippi River after departure — sail-away here is a slow, fascinating half-day cruise in itself. Stay on deck.

charleston_south_carolina_usa.blurb: Charleston's terminal opens onto one of America's best-preserved historic cities — antebellum mansions, Rainbow Row's pastels and the palmetto-lined Battery are all on foot. It's a homeport worth arriving early for.
miss: Rainbow Row and the Battery promenade
The City Market's sweetgrass basket weavers
A carriage tour's gossip-rich history loop
charleston_south_carolina_usa.tip: She-crab soup and shrimp-and-grits are the local orders — book dinner ahead, because Charleston's restaurant scene fills up even midweek.

baltimore.blurb: Baltimore's cruise terminal is an industrial locale a short ride from the Inner Harbor, where the National Aquarium, historic warships and crab houses cluster around the water. Fort McHenry — birthplace of the US anthem — guards the approach.
miss: The National Aquarium's blacktip reef
Fort McHenry, of Star-Spangled Banner fame
Fell's Point's cobbled pub streets
baltimore.tip: Your sail-away passes directly under the Key Bridge site and past Fort McHenry — the harbour exit is Baltimore's best viewpoint, so linger on deck.

new_york_new_york_usa.blurb: Sailing from New York — Manhattan's west side or Brooklyn's Red Hook — comes with the greatest sail-away in cruising: down the Hudson, past the Statue of Liberty and under the Verrazzano. The city itself needs no introduction, just extra days.
miss: The Statue of Liberty from your own deck at sail-away
The High Line to Hudson Yards near the Manhattan terminal
Times Square once, then never again
new_york_new_york_usa.tip: Book the port side of the ship (or just stand there) leaving Manhattan — Lady Liberty passes close on the left and the skyline does the rest.

boston_massachusetts_usa.blurb: Boston's Flynn cruise terminal sits in the Seaport, ten minutes from the Freedom Trail's red brick line through 250 years of revolution — Faneuil Hall, Paul Revere's house and the USS Constitution. Compact, historic and very walkable once you're in town.
miss: The Freedom Trail from Boston Common to the North End
The USS Constitution — 'Old Ironsides' — afloat since 1797
Cannoli in the North End (join the longer queue)
boston_massachusetts_usa.tip: New England fall foliage cruises book Boston hotels solid in late September — reserve pre-cruise nights months out, not weeks.

portland-maine.blurb: Portland lands you straight into the Old Port's cobbled lanes of fish shacks, breweries and boutiques — the best small-city walk-off on the New England circuit. Lighthouses, lobster boats and Casco Bay islands fill anything longer.
miss: Portland Head Light, Maine's postcard lighthouse
A lobster roll — decide your butter-vs-mayo allegiance
The Old Port's boutiques and micro-breweries
portland-maine.tip: Portland Head Light is a short taxi away in Cape Elizabeth and beats every in-town view — go there first, eat lobster after.

saint-john-nb.blurb: Saint John, New Brunswick sits on the Bay of Fundy, home of the world's highest tides — the Reversing Falls rapids literally flow backwards twice a day. The Victorian uptown and the covered City Market start right at the terminal.
miss: Reversing Falls Rapids at the right tide time
The 1876 City Market's dulse and maple stalls
Hopewell Rocks' flowerpot formations on longer calls
saint-john-nb.tip: Check the tide table first — the Reversing Falls only 'perform' around the turn of the tide, and cruise mornings don't always line up.

halifax_nova_scotia_canada.blurb: Halifax pairs one of the world's great natural harbours with an easy waterfront boardwalk that starts at the terminal. The Citadel crowns the town, Peggy's Cove's lighthouse sits an hour away, and the Titanic story runs deep here.
miss: Peggy's Cove's granite-perched lighthouse
The Citadel's noon gun and kilted sentries
The Maritime Museum's Titanic gallery
halifax_nova_scotia_canada.tip: The Fairview Lawn Cemetery's Titanic graves are moving and rarely crowded — a short, inexpensive taxi detour most tours skip or rush.

quebec_city_canada.blurb: Québec City is the closest thing North America has to a walled European town, and ships dock directly beneath it. The funicular links the Petit-Champlain's stone lanes to the Château Frontenac's boardwalk above; Montmorency Falls waits just outside town.
miss: Terrasse Dufferin's boardwalk beside the Château
Petit-Champlain — the continent's oldest shopping street
Montmorency Falls, higher than Niagara
quebec_city_canada.tip: The funicular queue is worth skipping if you can manage the Breakneck Stairs — and October calls come with peak fall colour on the Laurentians.

montreal_canada.blurb: Montréal bookends the Canada–New England run, with the Old Port's terminal steps from Old Montreal's cobbles, the Notre-Dame Basilica's blue-lit interior and a food scene that argues loudly with the rest of the continent.
miss: Notre-Dame Basilica's aurora light show
Bagels vs smoked meat — settle it at St-Viateur and Schwartz's
Mount Royal's lookout over the city
montreal_canada.tip: If your cruise starts or ends here, add a night — Montréal is a stay-and-eat city, and the Jean-Talon market alone justifies the extra day.

nuuk_greenland.blurb: Nuuk is the world's smallest capital with a skyline — colourful blocks against raw mountains at the mouth of a vast fjord system. The National Museum's Qilakitsoq mummies and the old colonial harbour anchor a compact, strollable visit.
miss: The Greenland National Museum's 500-year-old mummies
The old Colonial Harbour and Hans Egede's church
A boat trip into the Nuuk fjord if time allows
nuuk_greenland.tip: Weather rules everything in Greenland — have a flexible plan and treat any sunny hour as the moment to do the outdoor thing immediately.

seattle.blurb: Seattle is the Alaska cruise gateway, and both terminals sit within reach of the essentials — Pike Place Market's fish throwers, the original Starbucks queue you can skip, and the Space Needle. Add a day for the full coffee-and-mountains effect.
miss: Pike Place Market early, before the crowds
The Chihuly Garden and Glass beside the Space Needle
The Great Wheel and waterfront oyster bars
seattle.tip: Pier 91 (most big ships) is a ride from downtown while Pier 66 is walkable — check which one you're on before planning embarkation morning.

vancouver.blurb: Vancouver's Canada Place terminal is the prettiest embarkation in North America — downtown on one side, seaplanes and mountains on the other, and Stanley Park's seawall starting a short stroll away. Alaska sailings begin with the Lions Gate Bridge overhead.
miss: Stanley Park's seawall by bike or foot
Granville Island's public market by mini-ferry
Gastown's steam clock and brunch scene
vancouver.tip: Sail-away under the Lions Gate Bridge is the trip's first highlight — be on deck, because the ship clears it by what looks like inches.

victoria-bc.blurb: Victoria is the classic evening call on Alaska round-trips — a few hours of British-flavoured charm around the Inner Harbour, the illuminated Parliament, and the Empress Hotel's ivy. Butchart Gardens is the excursion if your hours allow.
miss: The Inner Harbour and Parliament lights after dusk
Butchart Gardens (check timing against your call)
Fisherman's Wharf's float homes and fish and chips
victoria-bc.tip: Most calls here are short and in the evening — decide in advance between Butchart's gardens and the harbour stroll, because doing both is a squeeze.

whittier.blurb: Whittier is a working port in a spectacular dead-end fjord, reached through a one-lane shared rail-road tunnel — and almost everyone who lives here shares one building. It exists on itineraries as the rail gateway to Anchorage, Denali and the interior.
miss: The 26 Glacier Cruise from the small-boat harbour
Portage Glacier en route to Anchorage
Begich Towers — the town that lives in one building
whittier.tip: If you're ending your cruise here, the scenic rail to Anchorage beats the coach — same tunnel, better windows, and glaciers to the door.

seward_alaska.blurb: Seward sits at the head of Resurrection Bay with Kenai Fjords National Park on its doorstep — the tidewater-glacier and whale-watching boat trips from here are among Alaska's best. The Alaska SeaLife Center anchors the compact town.
miss: A Kenai Fjords cruise to Aialik or Holgate glacier
The Alaska SeaLife Center's rescued puffins and sea lions
Exit Glacier's walkable viewing trails
seward_alaska.tip: Turnaround days here are tight for the big boat tours — if Kenai Fjords is the dream, consider staying a night rather than squeezing it into embarkation day.

juneau_alaska.blurb: Alaska's capital has no roads in — only sea and sky — and its cruise dock lands you in a walkable downtown with the Mount Roberts Tramway rising straight from the pier. Mendenhall Glacier and humpback whale watching are the twin headline acts.
miss: Mendenhall Glacier and Nugget Falls
Whale watching on Auke Bay — humpbacks near-guaranteed in season
The Mount Roberts Tramway from the dock
juneau_alaska.tip: The city 'blue bus' shuttles to Mendenhall for far less than a tour — glacier first, then spend the saving on crab legs at Tracy's shack by the dock.

skagway_alaska.blurb: Skagway is the gold rush preserved — false-front streets run straight from the dock, and the White Pass & Yukon Route railway climbs 3,000 feet through the mountains the stampeders crossed on foot. Book the train; it's the point of the port.
miss: The White Pass & Yukon Route railroad
The Klondike Gold Rush historical park's free walking tour
The Red Onion Saloon's brothel-museum cheek
skagway_alaska.tip: White Pass trains sell out weeks ahead in summer — book before the cruise, and sit on the left going up for the drop-away canyon views.

ketchikan_alaska.blurb: Ketchikan calls itself the Salmon Capital of the World and greets you with Creek Street — a boardwalk of former brothels on stilts over a salmon stream. It also holds the world's largest collection of standing totem poles, minutes from town.
miss: Creek Street's stilted boardwalk (salmon run in late summer)
Totem Bight or Saxman Village's totem parks
Misty Fjords by floatplane if the budget stretches
ketchikan_alaska.tip: Rain is the local weather — locals don't use umbrellas and neither should you; a hood and quick-dry layers make the day comfortable regardless.

sitka_alaska.blurb: Sitka was Russian America's capital, and it remains the most atmospheric town on the Alaska circuit — onion-domed St Michael's Cathedral, totem-lined rainforest trails and a raptor rescue centre, all against a volcano-backed sound.
miss: Sitka National Historical Park's totem trail
The Alaska Raptor Center's rehabilitating eagles
St Michael's Russian Orthodox cathedral
sitka_alaska.tip: Most ships dock at Old Sitka Dock with free shuttles the five miles to town — factor the shuttle queue into your all-aboard arithmetic.

icy_strait_point_alaska.blurb: Icy Strait Point is a cruise port owned by the Native Huna Tlingit village of Hoonah, built around a restored salmon cannery — with the world's largest zipline dropping 1,300 feet off the mountain above. The whale watching here is the best in Alaska.
miss: Whale watching in Icy Strait — humpbacks all season
The ZipRider's 90-second mountain drop
The cannery museum and Tlingit cultural performances
icy_strait_point_alaska.tip: Whales are so reliable here that operators guarantee sightings — if you're only doing one whale trip on the whole cruise, spend it at this port.

honolulu.blurb: Honolulu calls balance the unmissable weight of Pearl Harbor against Waikiki's surf and Diamond Head's crater rim walk. The Aloha Tower terminal sits between the two — downtown history one way, the beach the other.
miss: The USS Arizona Memorial at Pearl Harbor
Diamond Head's summit trail at first light
Waikiki Beach and a first surf lesson
honolulu.tip: Arizona Memorial tickets are released online in advance and go fast — reserve the moment your window opens rather than chancing standby.

cabo_san_lucas_mexico.blurb: Cabo is the Mexican Riviera's showpiece — the granite Arch at Land's End, sea lions, and Lover's Beach are all a short water-taxi hop from where the tenders land in the marina. It's a tender port, so the marina bustle starts immediately.
miss: The Arch (El Arco) by glass-bottom boat
Lover's Beach between two seas
Medano Beach's swimmable, bar-lined sand
cabo_san_lucas_mexico.tip: Water taxis to the Arch are cheap and constant from the marina — haggle politely, agree the price and photo stops before boarding, and you don't need a tour.

puerto_vallarta_mexico.blurb: Puerto Vallarta pairs a golden-sand bay with a genuinely charming old town — the Malecón's sculptures, the crowned church of Our Lady of Guadalupe and the Rio Cuale island market. The Zona Romántica's cobbles are the town at its best.
miss: The Malecón boardwalk's sculpture parade
The Zona Romántica and Playa Los Muertos pier
A panga boat to Yelapa or the southern coves
puerto_vallarta_mexico.tip: Local buses marked 'Centro' run from outside the port for pennies — the old town is fifteen minutes and a world away from the terminal malls.

mazatlan_mexico.blurb: Mazatlán's restored Centro Histórico is the Mexican Riviera's cultural sleeper — the Plazuela Machado's cafés, the Angela Peralta theatre and a 13-mile malecón patrolled by open-air pulmonía taxis. Cliff divers perform where the seawall bends.
miss: Plazuela Machado and the old town's restored streets
The cliff divers at El Clavadista
A pulmonía (open golf-cart taxi) ride along the malecón
mazatlan_mexico.tip: Ride a pulmonía at least once — agree the fare first — and time the cliff divers for when tour groups gather, as they perform when the tips assemble.

ensenada_mexico.blurb: Ensenada is Baja's port of call for short West Coast cruises — fish tacos allegedly born here, La Bufadora's blowhole thundering down the coast, and the Valle de Guadalupe wine country rising behind town.
miss: La Bufadora, one of the world's biggest blowholes
Fish tacos at the harbourside stands that invented them
A Valle de Guadalupe winery tasting
ensenada_mexico.tip: Hussong's Cantina claims the margarita's invention and looks every year of its age — one there for history, then follow the locals to the taco carts.

san-diego.blurb: San Diego's terminal sits on the downtown Embarcadero beside the USS Midway's flight deck — the Gaslamp Quarter, Little Italy and ferry to Coronado are all walkable. It's the easiest big-city homeport on the West Coast.
miss: The USS Midway museum next to the pier
Coronado's beach and grand old hotel by ferry
Balboa Park's museums and gardens
san-diego.tip: The Midway is literally beside the cruise terminal — even a two-hour pre-boarding visit works if you check bags at the port first.

los-angeles.blurb: The World Cruise Center at San Pedro is LA's cruise door, with the battleship Iowa moored alongside and the rebuilt West Harbor waterfront growing next door. Hollywood and Santa Monica are a committed ride away — plan LA days around traffic, not distance.
miss: The Battleship USS Iowa beside the terminal
The Korean Bell and Point Fermin's clifftop views nearby
Santa Monica Pier if you have a full pre-cruise day
los-angeles.tip: On embarkation day, stay near the port, not in Hollywood — the 110 freeway at the wrong hour has sunk more boarding schedules than weather ever has.

san-francisco.blurb: San Francisco's ships berth along the Embarcadero near Fisherman's Wharf, with the sea lions of Pier 39, cable cars climbing from the turnaround and Alcatraz out in the bay. Sailing under the Golden Gate is the harbour's mic-drop.
miss: Alcatraz — book weeks ahead, it sells out
A cable car over the hills to Union Square
The Ferry Building's food hall
san-francisco.tip: Sail-away under the Golden Gate Bridge is the single best deck moment in US cruising — it's usually cold and windy up there, so take a layer, not just a phone.

rio-de-janeiro.blurb: Rio needs no sales pitch — Christ the Redeemer, Sugarloaf's cable car and Copacabana's mosaic promenade deliver exactly what the postcards promise. The Pier Mauá terminal sits by the revitalised port district and its striking Museum of Tomorrow.
miss: Christ the Redeemer by cog train
Sugarloaf's two-stage cable car at golden hour
Copacabana and Ipanema's beach promenade
rio-de-janeiro.tip: Do Corcovado early — clouds swallow the statue by many afternoons — and use official taxis or apps rather than street hails, keeping valuables minimal.

santos-sao-paulo.blurb: Santos is São Paulo's seaside and the busiest port in Latin America — but for visitors it means beach gardens that hold a world record, a coffee-boom heritage and Pelé's own museum in the club where he played.
miss: The beachfront gardens — the world's longest
The Coffee Exchange's stained-glass trading hall
The Pelé Museum near Santos FC's ground
santos-sao-paulo.tip: São Paulo city is 80km and heavy traffic away — unless you have a late departure, Santos itself is the better-value day.

ajaccio_corsica.blurb: Ajaccio is Corsica's capital and Napoleon's birthplace, a sun-warmed port of pastel façades, palm-lined boulevards and a citadel over the sea. The emperor is everywhere — his house, his statues, his name on the squares.
miss: Maison Bonaparte, Napoleon's birthplace
The old town market on Place Foch
A boat trip to the Sanguinaires Islands at sunset
ajaccio_corsica.tip: Ajaccio is compact and walkable — the Napoleon sites, cathedral and market are an easy morning on foot, leaving the afternoon for the beaches or a coastal boat trip.

amsterdam_netherlands.blurb: Amsterdam's cruise terminal sits a short walk or tram from the centre — a city of concentric canals, gabled merchant houses, world-class museums and bicycles by the thousand. The Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh and Anne Frank's house are the pillars.
miss: The Rijksmuseum's Dutch Masters
The Van Gogh Museum
A canal cruise through the UNESCO ring
amsterdam_netherlands.tip: Anne Frank House and the Van Gogh Museum sell timed tickets online well ahead and routinely sell out — book the moment you know your date, or you won't get in on a port day.

rotterdam.blurb: Rotterdam is the Netherlands' bold architectural showcase, rebuilt after the war into a city of cube houses, the market-hall Markthal and daring bridges. It's also the gateway to the classic windmills of Kinderdijk and to Amsterdam and Delft.
miss: The Cube Houses and the Markthal food hall
The windmills of Kinderdijk, a UNESCO site
Historic Delft or Amsterdam by fast train
rotterdam.tip: Kinderdijk's nineteen windmills are the quintessential Dutch sight and reachable by waterbus from the city — a lovely way to travel there. Amsterdam is also just 40 minutes by train if you'd rather.

antwerp.blurb: Antwerp is Belgium's diamond city and Rubens' home town, its grand Central Station alone worth the trip. A magnificent cathedral, a guild-lined Grote Markt and a cutting-edge fashion scene fill a walkable, characterful centre.
miss: The Cathedral of Our Lady and its Rubens altarpieces
The Grote Markt and Renaissance city hall
Rubens' House and the diamond district
antwerp.tip: Antwerp is very walkable and often overlooked — the old centre, cathedral and riverside are an easy day on foot. Brussels and Bruges are each about 45 minutes by train if you want more.

bruges_zeebrugge_belgium.blurb: Zeebrugge is the port for Bruges — a fairytale medieval city of canals, cobbled squares, a soaring belfry and some of the world's best chocolate. One of Europe's most perfectly preserved old towns, about 20 minutes inland.
miss: The Markt square and the climbable Belfry
A canal boat trip through the old town
Belgian chocolate, waffles and the Beguinage
bruges_zeebrugge_belgium.tip: Bruges is the whole point of this port — get there via the train from Zeebrugge or a shuttle, don't linger in the industrial harbour. Climb the Belfry early before the queue builds.

gothenburg.blurb: Gothenburg is Sweden's laid-back second city — a maritime hub of leafy canals, the grand Avenyn boulevard, a beloved old amusement park at Liseberg, and a fresh-seafood culture centred on the church-like Feskekôrka fish market.
miss: The Haga district's wooden houses and giant cinnamon buns
Liseberg amusement park
The Feskekôrka fish market and harbour
gothenburg.tip: Gothenburg's vintage trams make getting around easy and cheap — grab a day ticket. The cosy Haga quarter with its café culture is the most charming corner to aim for.

lyon.blurb: Lyon, France's gastronomic capital, is a river-cruise call in the heart of the city — a UNESCO Renaissance old town threaded with secret 'traboule' passageways, a hilltop basilica reached by funicular, and the bouchon bistros that define French comfort food.
miss: Vieux Lyon's traboules and Renaissance courtyards
The Fourvière basilica and Roman theatres above
A traditional bouchon lunch
lyon.tip: The traboules — hidden passages linking streets through buildings — are Lyon's secret; a walking tour reveals ones you'd never find alone. Take the funicular up to Fourvière and walk down.

vienna.blurb: Vienna, a river-cruise highlight on the Danube, is the imperial Habsburg capital — the vast Schönbrunn and Hofburg palaces, the Gothic St Stephen's Cathedral, coffee-house culture and the music of Mozart and Strauss in every square.
miss: Schönbrunn Palace and its gardens
St Stephen's Cathedral in the old centre
A slice of Sachertorte in a grand coffee house
vienna.tip: Schönbrunn is a little way from the centre and sells timed tickets — book ahead and go early. Then the old town's cathedral, palaces and cafés are all walkable together.

verona.blurb: Verona, the city of Romeo and Juliet, is an inland Veneto gem often reached on tours from the Venice-area ports — a pink-marble Roman arena still hosting opera, medieval squares, and Juliet's balcony drawing the romantics.
miss: The Roman Arena, a working opera amphitheatre
Juliet's House and its famous balcony
Piazza delle Erbe's frescoed market square
verona.tip: If your itinerary offers Verona as an excursion, the Roman Arena is extraordinary — and if opera is on during your call, an evening in the ancient amphitheatre is unforgettable.

southampton_uk.blurb: Southampton is Britain's cruise capital rather than a sightseeing stop — the port most UK sailings begin and end at. The medieval walls, the SeaCity Museum's Titanic gallery (she sailed from here) and the old town's pubs fill the hours before boarding.
miss: SeaCity Museum's Titanic story — the ship left from here
The medieval town walls and Bargate
Mayflower Park for the ship's-eye waterfront
southampton_uk.tip: Most people treat Southampton as pure embarkation, but London is 75 minutes by train and Winchester's cathedral just 20 — either makes a better pre-cruise day than the port itself.

dover_uk.blurb: Dover's white cliffs are the point — the cruise terminal sits beneath them, with the great medieval castle on the headland above and the Kent countryside behind. It's a turnaround port, and Canterbury's cathedral is the classic add-on.
miss: Dover Castle and its wartime tunnels
The White Cliffs clifftop walk to South Foreland lighthouse
Canterbury Cathedral, 20 minutes inland
dover_uk.tip: London is far enough (two hours) that it's a stretch on a port day — Canterbury is the smarter half-day, close and unmissable.

portsmouth.blurb: Portsmouth is naval Britain in one place — Nelson's HMS Victory, the Tudor Mary Rose and the Spinnaker Tower all cluster around the Historic Dockyard by the terminal. Gunwharf Quays' shops and the seafront round out the day.
miss: HMS Victory and the Mary Rose in the Historic Dockyard
The Spinnaker Tower's glass-floor view
Old Portsmouth's Spice Island pubs and sea wall
portsmouth.tip: The Historic Dockyard ticket covers multiple ships and museums and easily fills a day — buy the all-attraction pass, not single entries, if you're staying local.

falmouth.blurb: Falmouth sits on one of the world's largest natural harbours, a Cornish town of maritime history, subtropical gardens and the National Maritime Museum on the quay. The Tudor Pendennis Castle guards the estuary mouth.
miss: The National Maritime Museum Cornwall on the harbour
Pendennis Castle's headland views
Gyllyngvase Beach, a short walk from town
falmouth.tip: Cornwall's star attraction, the Eden Project, is 45 minutes away — doable on a long call, but Falmouth's own gardens and coast reward staying put on a short one.

liverpool_uk.blurb: Liverpool berths you right on the UNESCO waterfront, steps from the Albert Dock, the Beatles Story and the Royal Liver Building. It's one of Britain's great walk-off ports — music, museums and football heritage all within reach on foot.
miss: The Albert Dock and the Beatles Story
The Museum of Liverpool and the Three Graces
A Cavern Club pint where the Beatles played
liverpool_uk.tip: The Magical Mystery Tour bus books up on ship days — reserve ahead if the Beatles sites are your priority, or just walk the dock and Mathew Street for free.

belfast_northern_ireland.blurb: Belfast is Titanic's birthplace, and the striking Titanic Belfast museum stands on the very slipway where she was built. The city's Victorian centre, the Cathedral Quarter's pubs and the Giant's Causeway up the coast complete the picture.
miss: Titanic Belfast on the original slipways
The Giant's Causeway, an hour up the Antrim coast
A black-cab political mural tour
belfast_northern_ireland.tip: The Giant's Causeway is the headline but it's a full-day round trip — if you'd rather stay in the city, Titanic Belfast plus the Cathedral Quarter is a satisfying day on foot and by shuttle.

dublin_ireland.blurb: Dublin's port is industrial, but the city it serves is compact and walkable once you're in — the Book of Kells at Trinity, the Guinness Storehouse, and the Temple Bar lanes are all close together. Georgian doors and literary pubs are the texture between them.
miss: The Book of Kells and Trinity's Long Room library
The Guinness Storehouse's rooftop Gravity Bar
A pint and trad music in a proper pub, not Temple Bar's tourist strip
dublin_ireland.tip: The port shuttle drops near the centre; from there Dublin is a walking city. Book Book of Kells and Guinness tickets online in advance — both queue badly on ship days.

cobh_cork_ireland.blurb: Cobh is the rare Irish port where the ship docks right in the pretty town — a steep tier of colourful houses under St Colman's Cathedral. It was Titanic's final port of call, and that story plus Cork city inland fill the day.
miss: The Titanic Experience in the original White Star office
The 'Deck of Cards' colourful houses below the cathedral
Cork city and the Blarney Stone, 25 minutes away
cobh_cork_ireland.tip: Cobh is walkable and lovely in its own right, so you don't have to bolt for Cork — but if you want to kiss the Blarney Stone, take the earliest tour as queues build all day.

glasgow-greenock.blurb: Greenock is the gateway to Glasgow and the Clyde. The town itself is a working port, but Glasgow's Victorian grandeur, the burdened-with-riches Kelvingrove museum and Loch Lomond's bonnie banks are all within easy reach.
miss: Kelvingrove Art Gallery in Glasgow (free entry)
Loch Lomond's shoreline, 30 minutes away
Glasgow's Mackintosh architecture and West End
glasgow-greenock.tip: Trains run from Greenock into central Glasgow in about 45 minutes for a few pounds — far better value than a ship transfer if you're comfortable on public transport.

edinburgh_south_queensferry_scotland.blurb: South Queensferry sits beneath the three great Forth bridges, a short ride from one of Europe's most beautiful capitals. Edinburgh's castle, the Royal Mile and the Georgian New Town are the reason to be here.
miss: Edinburgh Castle on its volcanic crag
The Royal Mile down to Holyrood Palace
The view of the three Forth bridges from Queensferry
edinburgh_south_queensferry_scotland.tip: It's a tender port and Edinburgh is 30–40 minutes away, so time is tighter than it looks — prioritise the castle (pre-book) and the Royal Mile rather than trying to see everything.

edinburgh-leith.blurb: Leith is Edinburgh's historic port, now a foodie quarter with the Royal Yacht Britannia moored alongside. A tram or short ride carries you up to the castle, the Royal Mile and the Georgian New Town.
miss: The Royal Yacht Britannia at Ocean Terminal
Edinburgh Castle and the Royal Mile
Leith's own waterfront restaurants and bars
edinburgh-leith.tip: Docking at Leith is a real advantage over the Queensferry tender — the tram into the city is quick and frequent, so you get more Edinburgh time. Pre-book the castle.

invergordon_scotland.blurb: Invergordon is the gateway to the Scottish Highlands — Loch Ness, Culloden's battlefield, and whisky country all radiate from this small Cromarty Firth town. The port itself is workaday; the glens are the draw.
miss: Loch Ness and Urquhart Castle
Culloden battlefield's moving visitor centre
A Speyside or Highland distillery tour
invergordon_scotland.tip: Loch Ness is about an hour away — a tour or pre-arranged driver makes far more of the day here than lingering in Invergordon, which has little to detain you.

kirkwall_orkney_scotland.blurb: Kirkwall is the capital of Orkney, where Neolithic Britain outdoes Stonehenge — Skara Brae, the Ring of Brodgar and Maeshowe are older than the pyramids. The red-stone St Magnus Cathedral anchors the walkable town.
miss: Skara Brae's 5,000-year-old stone village
The Ring of Brodgar standing stones
St Magnus Cathedral in town
kirkwall_orkney_scotland.tip: The great Neolithic sites are spread across the Mainland island and don't connect by public transport — book a tour or driver, because they're the entire point of Orkney.

stornoway_scotland.blurb: Stornoway is the capital of the Isle of Lewis in the Outer Hebrides — Harris Tweed, the ancient Callanish standing stones and a Gaelic-speaking culture at Britain's windswept edge. Lews Castle overlooks the harbour town.
miss: The Callanish Standing Stones, older than Stonehenge
Lews Castle and its museum by the harbour
A Harris Tweed weaver's workshop
stornoway_scotland.tip: This is deep in Gaelic Scotland and everything runs at island pace — don't over-plan; Callanish plus the castle grounds is a full, unhurried day.

bergen_norway.blurb: Bergen is the gateway to the fjords and a delight in its own right — the UNESCO Bryggen wharf's crooked wooden merchant houses, the funicular up Mount Fløyen, and a fish market on the harbour. Rain is frequent and forgiven.
miss: The Bryggen Hanseatic wharf houses
The Fløibanen funicular up Mount Fløyen
The fish market on the harbourfront
bergen_norway.tip: Bergen is Europe's rainiest city — pack a waterproof and don't let a grey sky stop you; the funicular view is atmospheric even in mist, and locals never carry umbrellas.

stavanger_norway.blurb: Stavanger pairs a beautifully preserved white-wooden old town, Gamle Stavanger, with dramatic fjord scenery — the Lysefjord and its Pulpit Rock are the region's hiking prize. The ship docks right at the compact centre.
miss: Gamle Stavanger's cobbled lanes of white houses
A Lysefjord cruise beneath Pulpit Rock
The colourful Fargegata (Øvre Holmegate) street
stavanger_norway.tip: Climbing Pulpit Rock is a committing 4-hour round-trip hike — most cruisers should take the fjord sightseeing boat instead, which gives the cliff from below without the sweat.

flam_norway.blurb: Flåm is a tiny village at the head of the Aurlandsfjord, hemmed by sheer walls and waterfalls — and home to one of the world's most scenic railways, climbing to Myrdal through 20 tunnels. The setting does all the work.
miss: The Flåm Railway to Myrdal
A fjord cruise on the narrow Nærøyfjord
The Stegastein viewing platform above Aurland
flam_norway.tip: The Flåm Railway sells out on ship days — book before you sail. Sit on the left going up for the Kjosfossen waterfall stop.

geiranger_norway.blurb: Geiranger sits at the head of the UNESCO-listed Geirangerfjord, arguably Norway's most spectacular — the Seven Sisters waterfall tumbles opposite and hairpin roads climb to eagle's-eye viewpoints. The sail in is the highlight of many cruises.
miss: The Dalsnibba skywalk, 1,500m above the fjord
The Eagle Road hairpins to Ørnesvingen viewpoint
The Seven Sisters waterfall from the water
geiranger_norway.tip: Be on deck for the approach — the sail into Geiranger past the waterfalls is as good as anything you'll pay to see ashore. It's a tender port, so book viewpoint tours early.

olden_norway.blurb: Olden lies at the head of the Nordfjord, the gateway to the Briksdal and Kjenndal glaciers that spill off the vast Jostedalsbreen ice cap. It's a small place in enormous scenery — the excursions into the glacier valleys are the day.
miss: The Briksdal Glacier and its milky glacial lake
Loen Skylift to the top of Mount Hoven
The Kjenndal Glacier valley drive
olden_norway.tip: The Loen Skylift is one of the world's steepest cable cars and delivers a jaw-dropping fjord panorama in minutes — a great option if glacier hiking feels too ambitious.

alesund_norway.blurb: Ålesund is Norway's Art Nouveau town, rebuilt in ornate style after a 1904 fire and set across islands between fjord and open sea. The view from Mount Aksla's steps over the turreted rooftops is the signature image.
miss: The Mount Aksla viewpoint (418 steps or a road)
The Art Nouveau centre and Jugendstil museum
The Atlantic Sea-Park aquarium
alesund_norway.tip: Climb the Aksla steps early before tour groups arrive — the town-and-islands panorama at the top is Ålesund's defining view and best with morning light.

oslo_norway.blurb: Oslo docks you beneath the Akershus Fortress in a compact, design-forward capital — the Viking-heritage museums on the Bygdøy peninsula, the striking Opera House you can walk on, and Vigeland's sculpture park are the highlights.
miss: The new Munch Museum and the Opera House roof
Vigeland Sculpture Park's 200 figures
The Fram and Kon-Tiki museums on Bygdøy
oslo_norway.tip: Norway is expensive — a day pass on public transport (which includes the Bygdøy ferry) saves a lot over taxis and reaches the museums easily.

copenhagen_denmark.blurb: Copenhagen is a cyclist's capital of colourful quaysides and easy charm — Nyhavn's painted houses, the Tivoli pleasure gardens, and the small Little Mermaid on her harbour rock. Much of it connects on foot or by the excellent metro.
miss: Nyhavn's canal-side coloured houses
Tivoli Gardens, the storied pleasure park
A canal boat tour past the opera and Christianshavn
copenhagen_denmark.tip: The Little Mermaid is famously smaller than everyone expects — see her in passing rather than making a special trip, and spend the time saved in Nyhavn or Tivoli.

berlin_warnemunde_germany.blurb: Warnemünde is a charming Baltic beach resort in its own right — a wide white-sand beach, a lighthouse and a canal of seafood stalls — but it's also the (long) gateway to Berlin, three hours each way by train.
miss: Warnemünde's beach, lighthouse and Alter Strom canal
Rostock's Hanseatic old town, 20 minutes away
Berlin — only if you accept 6 hours of travel
berlin_warnemunde_germany.tip: Berlin is a 3-hour train each way, leaving barely 4 hours in the city — it's a punishing day. Unless Berlin is a must, Warnemünde's beach and nearby Rostock make a far more relaxed call.

kiel.blurb: Kiel is a turnaround port at the Baltic end of the Kiel Canal, a sailing city rebuilt after the war. It's more embarkation hub than sightseeing star, though the maritime museum, the canal locks and Lübeck's marzipan old town nearby give it substance.
miss: The Holtenau locks where the Kiel Canal meets the sea
Lübeck's UNESCO old town, 45 minutes away
The Laboe naval memorial across the fjord
kiel.tip: If you're embarking or disembarking here, medieval Lübeck is the outstanding side-trip — far more rewarding than Kiel itself and easily reached by train.

hamburg.blurb: Hamburg is a grand maritime city of canals, red-brick warehouses and the spectacular Elbphilharmonie concert hall on the water. The UNESCO Speicherstadt warehouse district and the raucous Reeperbahn are its contrasting faces.
miss: The Elbphilharmonie's public viewing plaza
The Speicherstadt warehouse district and Miniatur Wunderland
A harbour boat tour of the working port
hamburg.tip: The Elbphilharmonie's Plaza viewing level is free but needs a timed ticket — grab one on arrival, then explore the Speicherstadt canals right beside it.

gdansk_gdynia_poland.blurb: Ships dock at Gdynia, but the prize is Gdańsk — a jaw-droppingly reconstructed Hanseatic old town of tall merchant houses, amber shops and the Long Market. The WWII and Solidarity history here is world-significant.
miss: Gdańsk's Long Market and Neptune's Fountain
The Museum of the Second World War
Amber shopping on Mariacka Street
gdansk_gdynia_poland.tip: Gdańsk is about 30 minutes from Gdynia by frequent commuter train — cheap and easy, and the old town is compact enough to see comfortably on foot once there.

stockholm_sweden.blurb: Stockholm spreads across 14 islands where lake meets Baltic, and the sail through its archipelago is a highlight in itself. The medieval Gamla Stan old town and the astonishing salvaged warship Vasa are the must-sees.
miss: The Vasa Museum's intact 17th-century warship
Gamla Stan's medieval lanes and palace
A hop across the water to Djurgården's museums
stockholm_sweden.tip: Depending on your ship's size you'll dock at Stadsgården (walkable) or further out at Nynäshamn (an hour away by train) — check which, as it changes the whole day's planning.

helsinki_finland.blurb: Helsinki is a clean-lined design capital where neoclassical and modern meet — the white Lutheran Cathedral over Senate Square, the rock-hewn Temppeliaukio church, and the harbourside market square with its food and craft stalls.
miss: The Rock Church (Temppeliaukio) carved into granite
Senate Square and the white Cathedral
The harbour market and a ferry to Suomenlinna fortress
helsinki_finland.tip: The sea fortress of Suomenlinna is a short public ferry from the market square and a lovely couple of hours — cheaper and more atmospheric than most organised tours.

tallinn_estonia.blurb: Tallinn has one of Europe's best-preserved medieval old towns — turreted walls, cobbled lanes and Gothic spires, all a short walk from the pier. Toompea hill's viewpoints look over the red rooftops to the sea.
miss: The medieval old town and Town Hall Square
Toompea hill's Kohtuotsa viewing platform
Alexander Nevsky Cathedral's onion domes
tallinn_estonia.tip: The old town is completely walkable from the port (about 15 minutes) — no need for a tour or transfer. Go straight up to Toompea first for the rooftop views before crowds build.

riga_latvia.blurb: Riga is the largest Baltic capital, its old town a UNESCO mix of medieval guildhalls and the world's finest concentration of Art Nouveau architecture. The Central Market's zeppelin-hangar halls are a browse in themselves.
miss: The Art Nouveau district on Alberta Street
The House of the Blackheads on Town Hall Square
Riga Central Market in the old zeppelin hangars
riga_latvia.tip: The old town is walkable from the berth; make time to wander north to the Art Nouveau quarter, which most day-trippers miss and which is Riga's real signature.

klaipeda_lithuania.blurb: Klaipeda is Lithuania's port city, with a German-flavoured old town of half-timbered courtyards from its centuries as Prussian Memel. Across the lagoon lies the extraordinary Curonian Spit, a UNESCO landscape of towering sand dunes.
miss: The Curonian Spit's giant dunes by ferry
The old town's Theatre Square and sculptures
The Lithuanian Sea Museum on the spit
klaipeda_lithuania.tip: The Curonian Spit is the unmissable half-day — take the passenger ferry across and a shuttle to Nida's dunes; it's a landscape found nowhere else in Europe.

reykjavik_iceland.blurb: Reykjavik is the base for Iceland's greatest hits — the Golden Circle's geysers and waterfalls, the Blue Lagoon's milky spa waters, and a compact, colourful capital topped by the rocket-like Hallgrímskirkja church.
miss: The Golden Circle: Þingvellir, Geysir and Gullfoss
The Blue Lagoon geothermal spa
Hallgrímskirkja's tower view over the coloured roofs
reykjavik_iceland.tip: The Blue Lagoon requires pre-booked timed entry and sits near the airport, not the city — decide early between it and the Golden Circle, as doing both in one port day is a rush.

akureyri_iceland.blurb: Akureyri is the capital of Iceland's north, a small town at the head of a long fjord and the gateway to the Lake Mývatn region — thundering Goðafoss, bubbling mud pools and the whale-rich waters off Húsavík.
miss: Goðafoss, the 'waterfall of the gods'
Lake Mývatn's craters and geothermal fields
Whale watching from nearby Húsavík
akureyri_iceland.tip: The Mývatn sights are 60–90 minutes out and worth the drive — but Goðafoss alone is only 40 minutes and stuns, if you'd rather keep the day shorter.

isafjordur_iceland.blurb: Ísafjörður is the heart of Iceland's remote Westfjords, a tiny town wrapped by steep flat-topped mountains and still water. It's raw, end-of-the-world scenery — Arctic foxes, old fishing heritage and the vast Hornstrandir wilderness nearby.
miss: The Westfjords Heritage Museum on the old harbour
A boat or hike toward the Hornstrandir reserve
The dramatic fjord-and-mountain setting itself
isafjordur_iceland.tip: This is one of Iceland's most weather-dependent ports and calls are sometimes cancelled by swell — if you make it ashore, just soak in the setting; the scenery is the attraction.

lisbon_portugal.blurb: Lisbon tumbles over seven hills above the Tagus, a city of yellow trams, tiled façades and pastel light. The Belém district's Manueline monastery and custard tarts, and the castle-topped Alfama, are the essentials.
miss: Belém's Jerónimos Monastery and the original pastéis
Tram 28 through the Alfama's steep lanes
São Jorge Castle's viewpoint over the city
lisbon_portugal.tip: Tram 28 is iconic but pickpocket-notorious and often jammed — ride it early, keep your bag close, or walk the Alfama instead for the same views at your own pace.

porto.blurb: Porto is Portugal's soulful second city, its steep riverfront Ribeira district stacked above the Douro and lined with the port-wine lodges that made its name. The tiled São Bento station and Lello bookshop are photographer's favourites.
miss: A port-wine lodge tasting in Vila Nova de Gaia
The Ribeira riverfront and Dom Luís I Bridge
Livraria Lello's spiral-stair bookshop
porto.tip: Cross the Dom Luís bridge's upper deck on foot for the classic Ribeira view, then descend to the Gaia lodges for a tasting — the two together are the essential Porto afternoon.

porto_leixoes_portugal.blurb: Leixões is Porto's cruise port, a short ride from the city's steep riverfront charms. The Ribeira district, the port-wine lodges across the Douro and the ornate São Bento station are all reasons to head straight into town.
miss: Port-wine tasting in the Gaia lodges
The Ribeira and the Dom Luís I Bridge
Livraria Lello and São Bento's tiled hall
porto_leixoes_portugal.tip: It's about 20–30 minutes into central Porto — take the shuttle or a taxi and spend your time in the city rather than around the industrial port at Leixões.

vigo_spain.blurb: Vigo is Galicia's largest city, a working fishing port famous for its oysters — shucked fresh on Rúa Pescadería. It's also the gateway to Santiago de Compostela's great pilgrim cathedral and the paradise beaches of the Cíes Islands.
miss: Oysters on Rúa das Ostras (Oyster Street)
Santiago de Compostela Cathedral, an hour away
The old town (Casco Vello) lanes
vigo_spain.tip: Santiago is a fine but full-day excursion; if you'd rather stay local, the old town plus a plate of Galician oysters and Albariño is a very Vigo way to spend the call.

la_coruna_spain.blurb: A Coruña is a Galician port wrapped by sea on almost every side, crowned by the Tower of Hercules — the oldest working Roman lighthouse on earth. Glass-galleried houses line the marina and, again, Santiago is within reach.
miss: The Roman Tower of Hercules lighthouse
The glass-galleried Marina façades
Santiago de Compostela, 45 minutes inland
la_coruna_spain.tip: The Tower of Hercules and the seafront promenade make an easy, scenic walking day — save the Santiago run for a longer call, as it eats most of your hours.

bilbao_spain.blurb: Bilbao transformed itself around Frank Gehry's titanium Guggenheim, one of the buildings of the age. Beyond it lies an atmospheric Basque old town, the Siete Calles, and pintxos bars that rival San Sebastián's.
miss: The Guggenheim Museum's architecture and Jeff Koons' Puppy
Pintxos-crawling the Casco Viejo old town
The Ribera food market on the river
bilbao_spain.tip: Even if modern art isn't your thing, the Guggenheim's exterior and riverside setting are free to enjoy — then dive into the old town for a pintxos lunch, Bilbao's real joy.

gibraltar.blurb: Gibraltar is a peculiar slice of Britain at the tip of Iberia — red pillar boxes and fish-and-chips beneath a towering limestone Rock. The cable car climbs to the summit, the Barbary macaques and views across to Africa.
miss: The cable car to the top of the Rock
The Barbary macaques — Europe's only wild monkeys
St Michael's Cave and the WWII Great Siege Tunnels
gibraltar.tip: The macaques will grab bags and food — keep both zipped and hidden. A Rock tour by minibus covers cave, tunnels and apes efficiently if the cable car queue is long.

cadiz_spain.blurb: Cádiz claims to be Western Europe's oldest city, a sea-wrapped tangle of narrow lanes, golden cathedral dome and Atlantic light. It's walkable and atmospheric — and the classic gateway to Seville and the sherry town of Jerez.
miss: The golden-domed Cathedral and its bell tower
The old town's plazas and Genovés gardens
Seville or Jerez inland for a bigger day
cadiz_spain.tip: Cádiz itself is a wonderful walking city, but Seville (90 minutes) tempts many — decide before you dock, because you can do one properly, not both.

seville-cadiz.blurb: Seville is the inland Andalusian jewel reached from the port of Cádiz — the vast Gothic cathedral with its Giralda tower, the Moorish Alcázar palace and the flamenco heart of Triana. It's a committed but rewarding day out.
miss: The Cathedral and Giralda tower climb
The Real Alcázar's palace and gardens
Plaza de España's tiled grandeur
seville-cadiz.tip: Seville is 90 minutes from Cádiz each way — book a tour or the fast train and accept the day is mostly Seville. The Alcázar sells out, so reserve entry ahead.

malaga_spain.blurb: Málaga has shed its package-holiday image to become a cultured Andalusian city — Picasso's birthplace, a hilltop Moorish Alcazaba fortress, and a palm-lined port promenade of galleries and tapas. Granada's Alhambra tempts inland.
miss: The Alcazaba and Gibralfaro castle above the city
The Picasso Museum in the old town
The Muelle Uno port promenade and cathedral
malaga_spain.tip: Granada's Alhambra is a bucket-list day but a long haul with pre-booked timed tickets that sell out weeks ahead — for a relaxed call, Málaga's own Moorish fortresses are superb and step-free from the port.

cartagena-spain.blurb: Cartagena is a Spanish naval city with a spectacular Roman past — a 2,000-year-old theatre only rediscovered in 1988 sits in the heart of a walkable centre of Modernista façades and a harbour castle.
miss: The restored Roman Theatre and its museum
The Modernista buildings on Calle Mayor
The Naval Museum and harbour ramparts
cartagena-spain.tip: Everything of note is within a compact, flat old town right by the berth — this is a rare Med port that needs no transport at all, just comfortable shoes.

alicante_spain.blurb: Alicante is a sunny Costa Blanca city beneath the Santa Bárbara castle, with a palm-lined marble esplanade, a good town beach and the colourful old quarter of Santa Cruz climbing the castle hill.
miss: Santa Bárbara Castle's panoramic summit
The Explanada de España's wavy marble promenade
The Santa Cruz barrio's flower-decked lanes
alicante_spain.tip: A lift inside the hill carries you up to the castle for a few euros — far easier than the climb, and the view over city and sea is the best in Alicante.

valencia_spain.blurb: Valencia is the birthplace of paella and home to the futuristic City of Arts and Sciences, a complex of gleaming white forms in a drained riverbed park. The medieval old town with its silk exchange and central market is the counterpoint.
miss: The City of Arts and Sciences and its aquarium
The Central Market and La Lonja silk exchange
Authentic paella in its home city
valencia_spain.tip: The port is a few kilometres from the centre — take the shuttle or metro. And eat paella at lunch, as Valencians do; it's traditionally a midday dish, not an evening one.

palma_de_mallorca_spain.blurb: Palma is dominated by La Seu, a honey-stone Gothic cathedral rising straight from the seafront ramparts. Behind it lies a maze of a old town — courtyards, boutiques and tapas — with the Serra de Tramuntana mountains beyond.
miss: La Seu cathedral's soaring Gothic interior
The old town's patios and Arab baths
Bellver Castle's circular hilltop keep
palma_de_mallorca_spain.tip: The cathedral is a short walk or shuttle from the pier and stunning inside — go first, then lose yourself in the old-town lanes behind it. Valldemossa in the mountains is the pick for a longer day.

barcelona_spain.blurb: Barcelona is a Mediterranean heavyweight — Gaudí's still-rising Sagrada Família and mosaic-strewn Park Güell, the Gothic Quarter's medieval lanes, and La Rambla's human parade. One port day only scratches it.
miss: The Sagrada Família (pre-booked timed entry essential)
Park Güell's mosaic terraces
The Gothic Quarter and Boqueria market off La Rambla
barcelona_spain.tip: Sagrada Família and Park Güell both need timed tickets bought well ahead — they sell out, and turning up hoping to enter on a ship day ends in disappointment.

monaco_monte_carlo_monaco.blurb: Monaco packs grand-prix glamour into two square kilometres — the Monte-Carlo casino, the superyacht harbour, the pastel old town of Monaco-Ville around the palace, and the excellent oceanographic museum on its cliff.
miss: The Monte-Carlo Casino square (dress code inside)
The Prince's Palace and changing of the guard at 11:55
The Oceanographic Museum on the cliff edge
monaco_monte_carlo_monaco.tip: Monaco is tiny but steep — public lifts and escalators link the levels for free; use them rather than trudging up, and time the palace for the 11:55am guard change.

nice_villefranche_france.blurb: Villefranche-sur-Mer is a postcard Riviera fishing town in a deep-blue bay, and a tender ride from Nice, Monaco and Èze. Nice's old town, flower market and pebble Promenade des Anglais are all a short train hop away.
miss: Nice's old town and Cours Saleya market
The hilltop village of Èze
Villefranche's own harbour and citadel
nice_villefranche_france.tip: The coastal train from Villefranche is cheap and scenic, linking Nice, Monaco and beyond in minutes — a do-it-yourself Riviera day beats most bus tours here.

cannes_france.blurb: Cannes is Riviera glamour distilled — the Croisette promenade, the film-festival Palais with its red-carpet steps, and the old Le Suquet quarter climbing to a hilltop view. Antibes and Grasse's perfumeries are close by.
miss: The Croisette and the festival Palais steps
Le Suquet old town and its castle museum
A ferry to the peaceful Îles de Lérins
cannes_france.tip: For a break from the glitz, the short ferry to Île Sainte-Marguerite offers pine-shaded walks and quiet coves — a lovely contrast to the Croisette crowds.

toulon_france.blurb: Toulon is France's great naval harbour, less touristy than its Riviera neighbours and the better for it — a Provençal old town of fountains and markets, a spectacular cable-car up Mont Faron, and Marseille or the Verdon within reach.
miss: The Mont Faron cable car and panorama
The Provençal market on Cours Lafayette
The naval museum on the harbour
toulon_france.tip: Toulon is refreshingly authentic and walkable, with the Mont Faron cable car its standout — a cheap, quick ride to a huge view that few cruise crowds bother with.

marseille_france.blurb: Marseille is France's oldest and grittiest-grand city, built around the Vieux-Port with the golden Notre-Dame de la Garde watching over it. The restored Panier quarter and the gateway to Provence and Aix add depth.
miss: Notre-Dame de la Garde's hilltop basilica
The Vieux-Port and Le Panier old quarter
Aix-en-Provence, 30 minutes inland
marseille_france.tip: The port is industrial and a fair way from the centre — take the shuttle to the Vieux-Port. From there a little tourist train grinds up to Notre-Dame de la Garde, saving a steep climb.

le_havre_paris_france.blurb: Le Havre's UNESCO post-war concrete architecture is more interesting than its reputation suggests, but the port is best known as the gateway to Paris (2+ hours) and to the Normandy landing beaches and Honfleur nearby.
miss: Honfleur's picture-perfect harbour, 30 minutes away
The Normandy D-Day beaches and memorials
Auguste Perret's UNESCO rebuilt city centre
le_havre_paris_france.tip: Paris is a long 2h15 each way — thrilling but exhausting on a port day. Honfleur or the D-Day beaches make a more relaxed and arguably more memorable Normandy day.

cherbourg_france.blurb: Cherbourg, on the Cotentin peninsula, is home to the Cité de la Mer maritime museum — set in a grand Art Deco transatlantic terminal where the Titanic once called, now housing a decommissioned nuclear submarine to tour.
miss: La Cité de la Mer and its submarine Le Redoutable
The Titanic connection in the old ocean terminal
