The D-Day beaches and Bayeux Tapestry, further out
cherbourg_france.tip: The Cité de la Mer is right by the ship and genuinely excellent — an easy, weather-proof day. For history buffs, the D-Day beaches and Bayeux are the bigger draw an hour away.

bordeaux.blurb: Bordeaux is a rare port where the ship sails up the Garonne into the heart of a UNESCO city — grand 18th-century stone façades, the mirror-pool Miroir d'eau, and of course the wine, with the Cité du Vin museum and châteaux beyond.
miss: The Place de la Bourse and its reflecting Miroir d'eau
La Cité du Vin wine museum
A Médoc or Saint-Émilion vineyard tour
bordeaux.tip: You dock right in the city, so Bordeaux itself is walkable — but this is the one port where a half-day wine-château excursion is almost obligatory. Book Saint-Émilion if you can.

la-rochelle.blurb: La Rochelle is one of France's prettiest Atlantic ports, its old harbour guarded by medieval stone towers and ringed by arcaded streets and seafood restaurants. The vast Aquarium and the Île de Ré's beaches are close.
miss: The medieval harbour towers you can climb
The old town's arcaded shopping streets
La Rochelle Aquarium, one of Europe's best
la-rochelle.tip: The old port and walled towers are a short, flat walk from the berth — an easy, charming day on foot, with the aquarium a good rainy-weather backup.

genoa_italy.blurb: Genoa is a proud, gritty maritime republic-turned-city, its UNESCO Palazzi dei Rolli lining lanes so narrow they never see sun. The huge old port, redesigned by Renzo Piano, holds Europe's biggest aquarium.
miss: The Palazzi dei Rolli on Via Garibaldi
The Aquarium of Genoa at the old port
The caruggi — the labyrinthine medieval alleys
genoa_italy.tip: Genoa is also a gateway to the Cinque Terre and Portofino, but both are long day-trips — if you stay in the city, the Rolli palaces and the caruggi are an atmospheric, underrated day.

savona_italy.blurb: Savona is a Ligurian port and Costa Cruises' home base, with a handsome medieval core around the Priamàr fortress. It's also a springboard to the Riviera — Genoa, and the villages of the coast are within reach by train.
miss: The Priamàr Fortress on the waterfront
The old town's towers and cathedral
Genoa or the Riviera coast by train
savona_italy.tip: Savona is pleasant but modest — the frequent coastal train opens up Genoa in 45 minutes or the Riviera villages, making more of the day than the town alone offers.

la_spezia_italy.blurb: La Spezia is the gateway to the Cinque Terre — the five impossibly picturesque cliff-clinging villages that are the whole reason ships call here. The town itself is workmanlike; the coast is the magic.
miss: The five Cinque Terre villages by train
Portovenere's colourful harbour, just south
Riomaggiore and Vernazza for the classic views
la_spezia_italy.tip: Buy a Cinque Terre train card at La Spezia station and hop between the villages — it's the easiest way to see several. Go early; these tiny places are overwhelmed by midday.

livorno-florence-pisa.blurb: Livorno is Tuscany's port — the gateway to Renaissance Florence and the leaning tower of Pisa. The port city itself has canals and a superb seafood tradition, but nearly everyone heads inland to the art cities.
miss: Florence's Duomo, Uffizi and Michelangelo's David
Pisa's Leaning Tower and Field of Miracles
Livorno's own canals and cacciucco fish stew
livorno-florence-pisa.tip: Florence is 90 minutes each way — pick either Florence OR Pisa plus a Tuscan town, not both. Book Uffizi/Accademia tickets ahead; the queues without them devour a port day.

florence_livorno_italy.blurb: Reached from the port of Livorno, Florence is the cradle of the Renaissance — Brunelleschi's dome, the Uffizi's masterpieces, Michelangelo's David and the goldsmith-lined Ponte Vecchio, all in a compact, walkable centre.
miss: The Duomo and Brunelleschi's dome
The Uffizi Gallery (pre-book to skip the line)
Michelangelo's David at the Accademia
florence_livorno_italy.tip: It's 90 minutes from Livorno, so you get roughly 4–5 hours in the city — enough for the Duomo, one major gallery and a wander, if you've pre-booked entries. Don't attempt Pisa as well.

rome_civitavecchia_italy.blurb: Civitavecchia is the port for Rome, 80km away — the Colosseum, the Vatican, the Trevi Fountain and 2,500 years of history. It's a long but unmissable haul for a first-timer; repeat visitors sometimes explore closer Cerveteri or Tarquinia.
miss: The Colosseum and Roman Forum
The Vatican Museums and St Peter's
The Trevi Fountain and Pantheon
rome_civitavecchia_italy.tip: Rome is a committed day — the train takes 70–80 minutes each way. Pick a focused itinerary (ancient Rome OR the Vatican, not both), pre-book, and watch the all-aboard time religiously.

naples_italy.blurb: Naples is chaotic, historic and thrilling — birthplace of pizza, guardian of Pompeii, and gateway to the Amalfi Coast and Capri, all under the brooding cone of Vesuvius. The ship docks right by the walkable old centre.
miss: Pompeii or Herculaneum's frozen Roman cities
The Amalfi Coast or the island of Capri
Naples' own historic centre and true Neapolitan pizza
naples_italy.tip: So many great options radiate from here that the mistake is trying to combine them — choose one (Pompeii, Amalfi, Capri, or the city) and do it well. For the city itself, the archaeological museum houses Pompeii's best finds.

messina_sicily.blurb: Messina is Sicily's gateway across the strait from the mainland, best known for its astronomical clock's noon performance and as the launch point for Taormina's clifftop beauty and Mount Etna's smoking heights.
miss: Taormina's Greek theatre and clifftop views
Mount Etna's lava fields and craters
Messina's animated astronomical clock at noon
messina_sicily.tip: Taormina is the crowd-pleaser and only 45 minutes away — its Greek theatre framing Etna is one of Sicily's great sights. Be in the cathedral square at noon first if you want the famous clock show.

palermo_sicily.blurb: Palermo is Sicily at its most intense — Arab-Norman palaces and mosaics, baroque churches, street markets that feel like souks, and the mosaic-drenched cathedral of nearby Monreale. Grand, faded and gloriously alive.
miss: Monreale Cathedral's golden mosaics, just outside town
The Palatine Chapel and Norman Palace
The Ballarò or Vucciria street markets
palermo_sicily.tip: Monreale's mosaics are arguably Sicily's single greatest sight and only 20 minutes away — pair them with a walk through Palermo's markets for the full sensory hit.

cagliari_sardinia.blurb: Cagliari, Sardinia's capital, rises in tiers to the walled Castello district with sweeping views over the gulf. A long white beach, Roman remains and pink-flamingo lagoons sit within easy reach of the port.
miss: The hilltop Castello quarter and its bastion terrace
Poetto Beach's long white sands
The Roman amphitheatre and flamingo lagoons
cagliari_sardinia.tip: The old Castello district is a steep but rewarding walk up from the port; a lift eases part of the climb. Poetto Beach is a short bus ride if you'd rather swim than sightsee.

olbia_sardinia.blurb: Olbia is the gateway to Sardinia's glamorous Costa Smeralda — the emerald-water coves and celebrity marinas of Porto Cervo. The town itself is modest; the beaches and the granite-sculpted coastline are the reason to call.
miss: The Costa Smeralda's beaches and Porto Cervo
A boat trip to the Maddalena archipelago
Olbia's San Simplicio basilica in town
olbia_sardinia.tip: The best beaches need transport from Olbia — arrange a taxi or tour to the Costa Smeralda coves, as the town beach doesn't compare to what's a short drive north.

bari.blurb: Bari is the capital of Puglia, its atmospheric old town (Bari Vecchia) a maze where grandmothers make orecchiette pasta in the street. The basilica holds the relics of St Nicholas — Santa Claus himself — and the trulli of Alberobello lie inland.
miss: Bari Vecchia and the Basilica di San Nicola
Watching orecchiette made on Strada Arco Basso
The fairytale trulli houses of Alberobello
bari.tip: Bari's old town is compact and free to wander — see the pasta-makers and the basilica on foot, then decide if the trulli of Alberobello (an hour away) are worth the trip out.

trieste.blurb: Trieste is a grand, Habsburg-flavoured city at Italy's far northeast corner, its vast sea-facing Piazza Unità one of Europe's finest squares. Literary cafés, the clifftop Miramare Castle and a Central-European soul set it apart.
miss: Piazza Unità d'Italia, open to the sea
Miramare Castle on its promontory
A historic Viennese-style coffee house
trieste.tip: Trieste is a walkable, uncrowded pleasure — do the great square and the old town on foot, then take a short bus or boat to the fairytale Miramare Castle on the coast.

venice_italy.blurb: Venice needs no introduction — St Mark's Basilica and its square, the Grand Canal, the Rialto and a thousand bridges over silent water. Large ships now dock at Marghera with a transfer, but the city remains singular.
miss: St Mark's Basilica and the Campanile view
A vaporetto down the Grand Canal
Getting deliberately lost in the quiet back sestieri
venice_italy.tip: Check where your ship berths — big ships use Marghera with a shuttle/boat transfer into the city, adding time. Once in, the vaporetto down the Grand Canal is the best-value 'tour' in Venice.

ljubljana-koper.blurb: Reached from the port of Koper, Ljubljana is one of Europe's most charming small capitals — a car-free riverside centre of pastel façades, dragon-guarded bridges and a hilltop castle, all designed with the architect Plečnik's flair.
miss: The old town and Plečnik's Triple Bridge
Ljubljana Castle's funicular and views
The riverside cafés and central market
ljubljana-koper.tip: Ljubljana is about 90 minutes from Koper — well worth it, and the compact centre is easily walked. Many tours combine it with Lake Bled, but that makes for a very full day.

zadar.blurb: Zadar is a Dalmatian old town on a walled peninsula, layered with Roman ruins and Venetian churches — and two modern marvels, the wave-powered Sea Organ and the solar Sun Salutation on its waterfront.
miss: The Sea Organ playing with the waves
The Roman Forum and round St Donatus church
A day-trip to Plitvice Lakes' waterfalls
zadar.tip: Zadar's old town is walkable straight off the ship — save the sunset-famous Sea Organ for whenever you're there, as it plays all day. Plitvice's lakes are stunning but a long inland haul.

split_croatia.blurb: Split is built inside a Roman emperor's retirement palace — Diocletian's 1,700-year-old walls now enclose a living old town of cafés, apartments and shops. The waterfront Riva and the islands offshore complete it.
miss: Diocletian's Palace and its cellars
The cathedral bell tower's climb and view
The Riva promenade and Marjan hill
split_croatia.tip: The palace is the old town, and it's right at the berth — just walk in and wander. A guided hour helps decode the Roman layers, after which you can explore freely.

dubrovnik_croatia.blurb: Dubrovnik's walled old town is a marble-streeted marvel above the Adriatic — the 'Pearl of the Adriatic', and King's Landing to a generation of TV viewers. Walking the medieval walls is the essential, unforgettable experience.
miss: The circuit of the city walls
The marble Stradun and Rector's Palace
The cable car up Mount Srđ for the aerial view
dubrovnik_croatia.tip: Walk the walls early — they get scorching and jammed by midday, with little shade. The port is 3km from the old town, so use the shuttle or a taxi, not your feet, to get there.

kotor_montenegro.blurb: Kotor sits at the head of a fjord-like bay ringed by mountains, its medieval walled town climbing into a dramatic fortress on the cliff above. The sail in through the Bay of Kotor is one of the Med's most beautiful.
miss: The climb to San Giovanni fortress above the town
The walled old town's squares and cathedral
The Bay of Kotor sail-in from deck
kotor_montenegro.tip: The fortress climb is 1,350 steps and brutally hot after mid-morning — start early with water, or enjoy the walled town at sea level, which is a delight in itself.

corfu_greece.blurb: Corfu's UNESCO old town is a Venetian, French and British layer-cake — elegant arcades, two hilltop fortresses and a cricket-playing esplanade, all wrapped in Ionian green. Beaches and Achilleion palace lie beyond.
miss: The Old Fortress and the Liston arcade
The Achilleion Palace of Empress Sisi
Paleokastritsa's coves on the west coast
corfu_greece.tip: The old town is a short shuttle or 20-minute walk from the port and easily explored on foot — save transport for the beaches or the Achilleion if you want to venture out.

athens_piraeus_greece.blurb: Piraeus is the port for Athens — the Acropolis and its Parthenon crowning the city, the ancient Agora below, and the superb Acropolis Museum at its foot. The cradle of Western civilisation, about 30–40 minutes from the ship.
miss: The Acropolis and Parthenon
The Acropolis Museum's Parthenon gallery
The Plaka old quarter beneath the rock
athens_piraeus_greece.tip: The metro runs from Piraeus straight to the Acropolis area in about 30 minutes for a couple of euros. Go to the Acropolis first thing — the marble bakes and the crowds swell fast by late morning.

mykonos_greece.blurb: Mykonos is the Cyclades at their most glamorous — a dazzling white-and-blue maze of a town, the row of windmills above it, and Little Venice's houses standing in the sea. Chic, crowded and photogenic at every turn.
miss: The iconic windmills above the town
Little Venice's waterfront houses at sunset
Getting lost in Chora's whitewashed lanes
mykonos_greece.tip: Chora's lanes were deliberately built as a maze to confuse pirates and they'll confuse you too — that's the fun; just wander. Beaches need a bus or taxi from town.

santorini_greece.blurb: Santorini is the caldera showstopper — whitewashed towns clinging to volcanic cliffs high above a flooded crater, with Oia's blue domes the most photographed sight in Greece. The tender-and-cable-car access is the catch.
miss: Oia's blue domes and famous sunset views
The clifftop caldera walk from Fira to Firostefani
A boat trip to the volcanic hot springs
santorini_greece.tip: It's a tender port and the cable car from the old port to Fira gets huge queues both ways — go up early, and budget real time for the return line so you don't miss all-aboard.

heraklion_crete_greece.blurb: Heraklion is Crete's capital and the gateway to Knossos, the labyrinthine Bronze Age palace of the Minoans — Europe's oldest city. The town's archaeological museum holds the treasures found there.
miss: The Palace of Knossos and its frescoes
The Heraklion Archaeological Museum's Minoan finds
The Venetian harbour fortress and old town
heraklion_crete_greece.tip: Knossos makes far more sense paired with the archaeological museum, where the original frescoes and artefacts live — do the palace first, then the museum to see what filled it.

rhodes_greece.blurb: Rhodes has the largest inhabited medieval old town in Europe — a walled crusader city of the Knights of St John, with the cobbled Street of the Knights and a Grand Master's palace, all a short walk from the ship.
miss: The Palace of the Grand Master
The Street of the Knights
The hilltop Acropolis of Lindos, down the coast
rhodes_greece.tip: The walled old town is right by the port and free to wander for hours. Lindos and its clifftop acropolis are the big excursion — beautiful, but check the steep donkey-path climb suits you.

izmir.blurb: Izmir is Turkey's relaxed Aegean city and the gateway to Ephesus — one of the greatest classical sites in the world, its marble library and vast theatre astonishingly preserved. The city's seafront Kordon and bazaar add local colour.
miss: Ephesus's Library of Celsus and Great Theatre
The House of the Virgin Mary nearby
Izmir's Kemeraltı bazaar and Kordon promenade
izmir.tip: Ephesus is about an hour away and unmissable — go early to beat both heat and crowds. Combine it with the Terrace Houses (extra ticket) for the best-preserved Roman interiors.

kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.blurb: Kuşadası is the purpose-built gateway to Ephesus, the spectacular ruined Roman metropolis 20 minutes inland. The resort town itself has a bazaar, a causeway castle and plenty of carpet shops eager for your custom.
miss: Ephesus and the Library of Celsus
The Terrace Houses' Roman mosaics
The House of the Virgin Mary in the hills above
kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.tip: Ephesus is very close here, so you can go early and still have time for the town. Expect persistent but good-natured sales pressure in Kuşadası's bazaar — haggling is part of the game.

bodrum_turkey.blurb: Bodrum is Turkey's stylish Aegean resort, built around a Crusader castle that guards twin bays. The site of the ancient Mausoleum — one of the Seven Wonders — and a lively marina and bazaar give it substance beyond the beaches.
miss: Bodrum Castle and its Museum of Underwater Archaeology
The site of the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
The marina, bazaar and whitewashed old town
bodrum_turkey.tip: The castle and its shipwreck museum are the standout and right in town — an easy walk from the harbour. The town is very walkable, so you rarely need transport here.

istanbul_turkey.blurb: Istanbul straddles two continents and layers Byzantine and Ottoman glory — the Hagia Sophia, the Blue Mosque, Topkapı Palace and the labyrinthine Grand Bazaar, most clustered in walkable Sultanahmet. The new Galataport terminal is central.
miss: Hagia Sophia and the Blue Mosque facing each other
Topkapı Palace and its harem
The Grand Bazaar and Spice Bazaar
istanbul_turkey.tip: The Galataport terminal is walkable to Karaköy and a short tram from Sultanahmet's big sights. Dress modestly for the mosques (scarves for women, covered shoulders/knees) and check prayer-time closures.

limassol.blurb: Limassol is Cyprus's easygoing coastal city, with a restored old port and marina, a medieval castle where Richard the Lionheart married, and the ancient sites of Kourion and Aphrodite's legendary birthplace along the coast.
miss: The ancient theatre and mosaics of Kourion
Limassol Castle in the old town
Aphrodite's Rock (Petra tou Romiou) down the coast
limassol.tip: Kourion's clifftop Greco-Roman theatre is the island highlight near Limassol — pair it with Aphrodite's Rock on the same coastal road for an easy, scenic half-day.

valletta_malta.blurb: Valletta is a golden Baroque city built by the Knights of St John, packed onto a peninsula of honey-stone palaces and gardens above the Grand Harbour. St John's Co-Cathedral hides a Caravaggio; the harbour views are sublime.
miss: St John's Co-Cathedral and its Caravaggio
The Upper Barrakka Gardens' harbour panorama
The ancient silent city of Mdina nearby
valletta_malta.tip: A lift from the harbour whisks you up into the city, saving the climb. Time the Upper Barrakka Gardens for noon or 4pm to catch the saluting-battery cannon firing over the harbour.

funchal_madeira_portugal.blurb: Funchal, capital of subtropical Madeira, is a city of flowers, fortified wine and dramatic scenery — a cable car up to Monte, a hair-raising wicker toboggan ride back down, and levada walks through the green interior.
miss: The cable car to Monte and its tropical gardens
The Monte wicker toboggan run down the hill
A Madeira wine lodge tasting in the old town
funchal_madeira_portugal.tip: The classic combo is the cable car up to Monte, the gardens, then the wicker-sledge ride down — book nothing in advance, just do them in that order for the signature Funchal day.

lanzarote_arrecife_spain.blurb: Lanzarote is a volcanic moonscape shaped by the artist César Manrique, whose vision defines the island — the fire mountains of Timanfaya, a cactus garden, and a house built inside lava bubbles. Unlike anywhere else in the Canaries.
miss: Timanfaya's fire mountains and geothermal demos
César Manrique's Jameos del Agua lava caves
The camel rides and volcanic vineyards of La Geria
lanzarote_arrecife_spain.tip: Timanfaya is the island's wonder but is only accessible by the official coach tour inside the park — a ship excursion or organised trip is the practical way to see it.

las_palmas_gran_canaria.blurb: Las Palmas mixes a historic quarter where Columbus once prayed with one of Europe's best city beaches, the golden Playa de las Canteras. The dramatic dunes and mountainous interior of Gran Canaria lie beyond.
miss: Playa de las Canteras city beach
The Vegueta old town and Columbus House
The Maspalomas dunes in the island's south
las_palmas_gran_canaria.tip: Canteras beach is a short ride or walk from the port and genuinely excellent — a rare port where a proper beach day is easy. The old Vegueta quarter is the cultural counterweight.

santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.blurb: Santa Cruz de Tenerife sits beneath Spain's highest peak, the volcanic Mount Teide, whose cable car climbs toward a 3,700m summit through a Martian national park. The wavy Auditorio and old La Laguna add culture.
miss: Mount Teide's cable car and volcanic park
The historic UNESCO town of La Laguna
The Calatrava-designed Auditorio de Tenerife
santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.tip: Teide is the star but a long ascent — a tour is the easy way, and the cable car needs decent weather to be worth it. La Laguna is a lovely, cooler alternative closer to port.

agadir_morocco.blurb: Agadir is Morocco's modern beach resort, rebuilt after a 1960 earthquake — a long sandy bay, a hilltop kasbah ruin with the city's name spelled in the hillside, and a souk that offers a gentler taste of Morocco than the imperial cities.
miss: The clifftop Kasbah ruins and hillside inscription
Souk El Had, one of Morocco's largest markets
The beach promenade and camel rides
agadir_morocco.tip: Agadir is more resort than deep-Morocco; for atmosphere, the walled town of Taroudant or the Paradise Valley oasis inland make richer excursions than the beach strip.
